content:
# 2 Thessalonians Chapter 2 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 204-205 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2TH_002_page_001.jpg, 2TH_002_page_002.jpg

### The Coming of the Lord and the Man of Lawlessness

**1.** Now, brothers and sisters, about the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ and our being gathered together to meet him: we ask you

**2.** not to be easily unsettled in your thinking or thrown into panic — whether by some spirit, or by a message, or by a letter claiming to be from us — suggesting that the Day of the Lord has already come.

**3.** Do not let anyone deceive you in any way. That day will not come until the great rebellion takes place and the man of lawlessness is revealed — the one who is destined for destruction.

**4.** He will oppose and exalt himself above every so-called god or object of worship, so that he seats himself in God's temple, declaring himself to be God.

**5.** Do you not remember that I told you these things while I was still with you?

**6.** And you know what is currently holding him back, so that he will be revealed only when his time comes.

**7.** For the hidden power of lawlessness is already at work; but the one who now restrains it will continue to do so until he is removed from the way.

**8.** And then the lawless one will be revealed, whom the Lord Jesus will destroy with the breath of his mouth and wipe out by the sheer brilliance of his coming.

**9.** The coming of the lawless one will be accompanied by the working of Satan, displayed in all kinds of counterfeit miracles, signs, and wonders,

**10.** and in every sort of evil deception aimed at those who are perishing — because they refused to love the truth and so be saved.

**11.** For this reason God sends them a powerful delusion so that they will believe the lie,

**12.** so that all who have not believed the truth but have taken pleasure in wickedness will be condemned.

### Stand Firm in What You Were Taught

**13.** But we are always bound to give thanks to God for you, brothers and sisters loved by the Lord, because God chose you as firstfruits for salvation through the sanctifying work of the Spirit and through belief in the truth.

**14.** He called you to this through our proclamation of the good news, so that you might share in the glory of our Lord Jesus Christ.

**15.** So then, brothers and sisters, stand firm and hold fast to the traditions you were taught, whether by what we said in person or by letter.

**16.** Now may our Lord Jesus Christ himself, and God our Father, who loved us and by his grace gave us an eternal comfort and a good hope,

**17.** encourage your hearts and strengthen you in every good work and word.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verses 1-2 — "Do not be shaken":** Paul is responding to a crisis. Someone — through a prophetic utterance, a spoken message, or even a forged letter bearing Paul's name — has told the Thessalonians that the Day of the Lord has already arrived. They are in a state of alarm. Paul's first move is pastoral: calm down. His second move is theological: here is why that claim is wrong.

**Verses 3-4 — The man of lawlessness:** This is one of the most debated passages in the entire New Testament. Paul describes a figure who will lead a great apostasy and set himself up as God in God's own temple. The Sinaiticus reading has "man of lawlessness" (anomias) rather than the later variant "man of sin." Paul does not name this figure. He expects the Thessalonians to know who he means (v.5), but we do not. What is clear: before Christ returns, a final act of human rebellion against God must occur — a rebellion embodied in a single figure who demands divine worship.

**Verses 6-7 — The restrainer:** Paul refers to something (neuter, v.6 — "what is holding back") and someone (masculine, v.7 — "the one who restrains") that is currently preventing the lawless one from being revealed. Scholars have proposed the Roman Empire, the archangel Michael, the Holy Spirit, the preaching of the gospel, and Paul himself. The honest answer: we do not know. Paul's audience knew. We are overhearing half a conversation.

**Verse 8 — "The breath of his mouth":** The language echoes Isaiah 11:4. The lawless one may seem terrifying, but his defeat will be effortless. Christ does not wage a war against him — he simply speaks, and it is over. The contrast is deliberate: all of Satan's spectacular counterfeits are undone by a single breath.

**Verse 13 — "Firstfruits" (Sinaiticus reading):** Codex Sinaiticus reads "aparchēn" (firstfruits) rather than the Byzantine "ap' archēs" (from the beginning). This is a significant textual variant. "Firstfruits" implies the Thessalonians are the early harvest of a much larger salvation — a term Paul uses elsewhere (Romans 16:5, 1 Corinthians 16:15). This reading is considered by many scholars to be more likely original.

**Verse 15 — "Hold fast to the traditions":** The word "traditions" (paradoseis) here means teachings handed down — not cultural customs. Paul is making a striking claim: his oral instruction and his written letters carry equal authority. This verse is one of the key texts in debates about Scripture and tradition in church history.

**Verses 16-17 — Closing prayer:** Paul shifts from instruction to prayer. Notice the order: "our Lord Jesus Christ himself and God our Father." Christ is named before the Father — unusual, and a quiet testimony to how early Christians understood Christ's status. The prayer asks for two things: encouragement and strength. Theology that does not produce comfort and action has missed the point.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
